ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general coffee beverage class; no specific entry for Singaporean Kopi O Siu Dai. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Kopi O Siu Dai. A00LC (coffee, black, with sugar) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor given the beverage is black coffee with reduced added sugar.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34325006 refers to coffee as a substance; no specific code for Kopi O Siu Dai preparation variant exists.

preparation_method: sweetened with condensed sugar — reduced quantity

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard kopitiam cup of Kopi O Siu Dai is approximately 200 ml (200 g), the typical serving size at a Singapore coffee shop.

nutrient_source.title: Kopi O Siu Dai — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database contains a direct entry for Kopi O Siu Dai. All per-100g nutrient values are taken directly from this entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sweetened Coffee Beverages — analogous estimate based on sucrose GI and beverage carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Kopi O Siu Dai in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated using sucrose as the primary glycemic driver (GI 65 per Sydney GI Database) moderated by the low carbohydrate concentration in the beverage matrix (4.7 g/100 g). This is consistent with the GI range reported for lightly sweetened coffee beverages in peer-reviewed literature. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (low: 55 or below). gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (55 × 9.4 g available carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 5.17, rounded to 5.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar present in a beverage with zero dietary fibre; caffeine may transiently elevate blood pressure
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sugar-sweetened beverages including Kopi O Siu Dai and consider switching to Kopi O Kosong (unsweetened black coffee) to eliminate added sugar while retaining potential vasodilatory benefits of coffee polyphenols.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar (4.7 g/100 g, all carbohydrate as sugar) with zero dietary fibre; qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage under ADA guidelines
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kopi O Siu Dai is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage and is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the ADA advises avoiding SSBs to prevent postprandial glucose spikes, and patients should substitute Kopi O Kosong which contains no added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar contributes to cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly; low sodium (9 mg/100 g) is favourable but saturated fat from robusta coffee oils is present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened coffee beverages such as Kopi O Siu Dai is associated with increased cardiovascular risk through added sugar load; individuals with CVD should switch to Kopi O Kosong and limit daily intake to one to two cups to benefit from coffee's cardioprotective polyphenols without the sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; zero dietary fibre and no anti-inflammatory micronutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Kopi O Siu Dai due to its added sugar content which promotes inflammatory mediators, and should switch to Kopi O Kosong to access coffee's chlorogenic acid anti-inflammatory benefits without the sugar-driven inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kopi O Siu Dai is a traditional Singaporean black coffee beverage prepared in the kopitiam (coffee shop) style, using robusta coffee beans roasted with sugar and butter, brewed by pouring hot water through a cloth sock strainer. 'Kopi' means coffee in the Hokkien and Malay vernacular of Singapore, 'O' denotes black (no milk), and 'Siu Dai' (少甜, Cantonese) means 'less sweet', indicating that the barista adds a reduced quantity of sugar compared to the standard Kopi O preparation. The resulting beverage is a dark, slightly bitter, lightly sweetened black coffee with a robust, full-bodied flavour profile characteristic of robusta beans. Per 100 g, it provides 28 kcal, 0.2 g protein, 0.9 g fat, and 4.7 g carbohydrate entirely as sugar, with negligible dietary fibre and low sodium (9 mg). Despite the 'siu dai' (less sugar) designation, added sugar is still present, classifying this beverage as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under ADA and WHO guidelines. It is therefore not recommended for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or those seeking to reduce SSB intake for hypertension or cardiovascular disease management. Kopi O Kosong (no sugar) is the clinically preferred alternative. Kopi O Siu Dai is a staple of Singapore breakfast culture, typically consumed at hawker centres and kopitiams alongside kaya toast and soft-boiled eggs.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with an exact match for Kopi O Siu Dai at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using sucrose as the primary glycemic driver, clearly documented. Multilingual names are verified for Singaporean Chinese (Hokkien/Cantonese kopitiam context).
notes: Per-100g values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (exact match, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated at 0.6 g/100 g based on robusta coffee oil composition; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry — value is an approximation from USDA FoodData Central entry for brewed coffee (USDA SR Legacy #14209) scaled to the fat content reported by HPB. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for Kopi O Siu Dai are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. iron_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo). dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as confirmed by HPB Foodo — this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-contributing solids. The 'keto' dietary tag present in the input has been removed: Kopi O Siu Dai contains 4.7 g sugar/100 g (9.4 g per 200 g serving) which is inconsistent with ketogenic dietary requirements. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Kopi O Siu Dai exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI 55 is the upper boundary of the low GI category (low: 55 or below); gi_category is therefore 'low'. Glycemic load per serving = (55 × 9.4) / 100 = 5.17, rounded to 5.2.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to align with G5 requirements.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI value of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category per the University of Sydney GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this classification rationale explicitly.
